subtopic_detail: Suspected giant cell arteritis with new headache and jaw claudication
stem: An 81-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of new bitemporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication when chewing and one episode of transient visual blurring in the right eye 12 hours ago. She has no diabetes, no peptic ulcer disease and no infection. There is no rash and no symptoms of polymyalgia at presentation. Her visual acuity is normal today. There is tenderness over both temporal arteries with reduced pulsatility on the right.

data_table.Examination: Tender thickened right temporal artery, reduced pulse; visual acuity 6/6 bilaterally, fundi normal
data_table.Investigations: ESR 92 mm/hour, CRP 124 mg/L, Hb 108 g/L, platelets 462 x10^9/L, ALT 24 U/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily today and arrange urgent temporal artery biopsy within 7 to 14 days, plus aspirin 100 mg daily and bone and gastric protection
options.rationale: Suspected GCA with visual symptoms is started immediately on high-dose corticosteroids; biopsy yield is preserved for 1 to 2 weeks. Aspirin reduces ischaemic events; bone and PPI cover address steroid harms. (Source: BSR guideline on GCA management; eviQ general rheumatology)

options.text: Defer corticosteroid therapy until temporal artery biopsy has been performed and reviewed, even if biopsy is not arranged for several days into the working week
options.rationale: Delaying steroids risks permanent visual loss; biopsy yield is preserved for up to 2 weeks of treatment. (Source: BSR GCA guideline)

options.text: Apply topical ophthalmic prednisolone drops alone to the right eye with no systemic corticosteroid therapy until the biopsy result has been reviewed by the rheumatology team
options.rationale: GCA is systemic; topical therapy does not address arteritis. (Source: BSR GCA guideline)

options.text: Oral naproxen 500 mg twice daily as monotherapy for the headache with reassessment of inflammatory markers and clinical features in 4 weeks at the next planned review
options.rationale: NSAID monotherapy will not control vasculitis and exposes the patient to preventable visual loss. (Source: BSR GCA guideline)

options.text: Initiate subcutaneous tocilizumab as monotherapy without any concurrent oral corticosteroid therapy at the first visit before any biopsy is arranged or organised
options.rationale: Tocilizumab is a steroid-sparing add-on in relapsing or steroid-toxic GCA; not monotherapy at first presentation. (Source: BSR GCA guideline)

explanation.summary: Suspected giant cell arteritis with any visual feature is treated with high-dose corticosteroids before biopsy: oral prednisolone 40 to 60 mg daily for uncomplicated GCA, IV methylprednisolone pulse for vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks remains diagnostic; biopsy yield is preserved on early treatment. Aspirin 100 mg daily reduces ischaemic events, while calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonates and a PPI cover steroid harms. Tocilizumab is a steroid-sparing add-on for relapsers.
key_points: Start high-dose corticosteroids immediately on clinical suspicion; do not wait for biopsy
Aspirin 100 mg daily reduces ischaemic events in GCA
Biopsy yield is preserved for 1 to 2 weeks after starting steroids
explanation.pearls: Visual symptoms in suspected GCA prompt IV methylprednisolone 500 to 1000 mg daily for 3 days; otherwise oral prednisolone 60 mg daily, with biopsy within 14 days.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: rheumatology - giant cell arteritis

subtopic_detail: Myasthenic crisis with bulbar and respiratory failure
stem: A 56-year-old woman with seropositive myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine and azathioprine presents with worsening generalised weakness over 5 days following a chest infection. She now has dysphagia, dysphonia and dyspnoea. She has no fevers today. Her forced vital capacity has dropped from a baseline of 3.4 L to 0.9 L. Negative inspiratory force is reduced. She is unable to count to 20 in a single breath.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/76, HR 108, RR 26, SpO2 93% on room air
data_table.Examination: Bilateral ptosis, paradoxical breathing, weak cough, gag reflex reduced
data_table.Investigations: FVC 0.9 L, NIF -22 cmH2O, anti-AChR antibody historically positive, CXR right basal opacity, ABG shows hypercapnia (pCO2 56 mmHg)

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Increase pyridostigmine fivefold and discharge home with a community nurse review after 24 hours, recommending early follow-up with the treating neurologist
options.rationale: Increasing pyridostigmine in crisis risks cholinergic crisis and does not address respiratory failure; admission is mandatory. (Source: Australian Myasthenic Association clinician resources; eTG Neurology)
source_refs: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management

options.text: ICU admission, prepare for non-invasive then invasive ventilation, treat the precipitating chest infection, and initiate intravenous immunoglobulin or plasma exchange
options.rationale: Myasthenic crisis with FVC under 1 L and hypercapnia requires ICU-level airway support, treatment of precipitants, and rapid immunomodulation with IVIG or plasma exchange. (Source: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management)
source_refs: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management

options.text: Edrophonium intravenous bolus 10 mg as a diagnostic test before any further therapy is considered, with no ICU referral and no escalation of monitoring in this presentation
options.rationale: Edrophonium is no longer routine in known seropositive disease and does not address respiratory failure. (Source: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management)
source_refs: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management

options.text: Oral prednisone titrated outpatient over 6 weeks as the only adjustment, with reassurance and home spirometry monitoring rather than admission for ventilatory support and immunomodulation
options.rationale: Slow steroid titration cannot rescue acute crisis; early steroid up-titration also risks transient worsening. (Source: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management)
source_refs: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management

options.text: Single dose intravenous lorazepam 2 mg for anxiety and supportive observation only on the general ward, with no airway escalation arranged or planned at this current encounter
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen ventilatory drive and risk respiratory arrest in neuromuscular failure. (Source: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management)
source_refs: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management

explanation.summary: Myasthenic crisis is bulbar or respiratory failure on a background of myasthenia gravis. Bedside markers include FVC under 1 L (or under 15 mL/kg), single-breath count under 20 and rising pCO2. Management is ICU admission for ventilatory support (NIV trialled cautiously, intubation when paradoxical breathing or rising pCO2 develops), treatment of triggers (infection, drugs such as aminoglycosides), and rapid immunomodulation with IVIG 2 g/kg over 5 days or plasma exchange. Steroid up-titration is initiated under cover of immunomodulation given the risk of transient worsening.
key_points: FVC under 1 L or single-breath count under 20 signals impending respiratory failure
IVIG 2 g/kg over 5 days or plasma exchange is the rescue therapy of choice
Aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides and magnesium can precipitate or worsen crisis
explanation.pearls: Single-breath count under 20 or FVC below 15 mL/kg in known myasthenia signals impending respiratory failure; mobilise IVIG or plasma exchange and prepare the airway team.

sources.label: AAN guideline on myasthenia gravis management

subtopic_detail: Clinically isolated syndrome with MRI dissemination meeting McDonald criteria
stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with a 7-day history of painful loss of vision in the right eye and impaired colour perception. Examination confirms right optic neuritis with a relative afferent pupillary defect. She has no prior episodes of neurological symptoms. MRI brain and orbits show right optic nerve enhancement plus three periventricular T2 lesions and one juxtacortical lesion. Both gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions are present. CSF shows two unique oligoclonal bands.

data_table.Examination: Right visual acuity 6/24, RAPD present, normal fundi; remainder of cranial nerves and limbs normal
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: dissemination in space (periventricular plus juxtacortical) and time (enhancing plus non-enhancing) present; CSF oligoclonal bands positive; aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold all disease-modifying therapy and reassess in 12 months with a repeat clinical examination and follow-up MRI, with no further specific action between visits
options.rationale: Active disease meeting McDonald criteria warrants prompt DMT initiation; delay accrues disability. (Source: MS International Federation; MSAANZ DMT consensus)

options.text: Apply five sessions of plasma exchange immediately for this first attack of optic neuritis without any further investigations or commencement of long-term disease-modifying therapy at this point
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is reserved for severe steroid-refractory attacks; it is not first-line acute or maintenance therapy for typical optic neuritis. (Source: MSAANZ DMT consensus)

options.text: Apply the McDonald 2017 criteria, diagnose relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, treat the acute attack with high-dose methylprednisolone, and initiate disease-modifying therapy
options.rationale: Dissemination in space and time on the first MRI satisfies McDonald 2017 criteria and supports diagnosing relapsing-remitting MS, with acute high-dose steroids and prompt DMT to reduce relapse and disability accrual. (Source: McDonald 2017 criteria; MSAANZ DMT consensus)

options.text: Steroid pulse for the attack and immediate cessation of all follow-up plans with no specific disease-modifying therapy initiated and no further specialist review arranged at this time
options.rationale: DMT initiation reduces relapse rate, MRI activity and long-term disability; discharge without DMT misses the window of opportunity. (Source: MSAANZ DMT consensus)

options.text: Wait for a second clinical attack before any treatment is initiated, with no acute corticosteroid for the current optic neuritis and no follow-up MRI planned at this time
options.rationale: Modern McDonald 2017 criteria allow diagnosis after the first attack when imaging shows dissemination in space and time; waiting harms outcomes. (Source: McDonald 2017 criteria)

explanation.summary: Clinically isolated syndrome with MRI findings that satisfy dissemination in space and time meets McDonald 2017 criteria for relapsing-remitting MS. Acute optic neuritis is treated with high-dose methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily for 3 to 5 days; recovery is typically excellent. Early initiation of a disease-modifying therapy (such as fingolimod, ocrelizumab, natalizumab or platform injectable agents per PBS criteria) reduces relapse rate, MRI activity and long-term disability accrual. AQP4 and MOG antibodies are checked to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD.
key_points: McDonald 2017 allows MS diagnosis after a first attack when dissemination in space and time are present on MRI
Treat acute optic neuritis with methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily for 3 to 5 days
Initiate DMT promptly to reduce long-term disability accrual
explanation.pearls: A first demyelinating attack with periventricular and juxtacortical lesions and concurrent gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI satisfies dissemination in space and time under McDonald 2017.

subtopic_detail: Acute chest syndrome in adult sickle cell disease
stem: A 27-year-old man with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents with 24 hours of fever, productive cough and pleuritic chest pain. He has been admitted twice in the last year for vaso-occlusive crises. His usual hydroxyurea has been taken regularly. Chest radiograph now shows a new right lower lobe infiltrate. Pulse oximetry shows hypoxia on room air. He has no penicillin allergy. He has had pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccinations.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/74, HR 116, RR 26, SpO2 90% on room air, Temp 38.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Right basal crackles and dullness to percussion; mild jaundice; spleen not palpable
data_table.Investigations: Hb 76 g/L (baseline 92), reticulocytes 11 percent, WCC 14.6 x10^9/L, platelets 388 x10^9/L, LDH 720 U/L, bilirubin 56 micromol/L; CXR confirms right lower lobe infiltrate

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management bundle?

options.text: Exchange transfusion is contraindicated in adults with sickle cell disease, and management is limited to oxygen, analgesia and oral antibiotics on the general ward only
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is a key salvage therapy in severe acute chest syndrome; it is not contraindicated. (Source: BSH guidelines for the management of sickle cell disease; HSANZ paediatric and adult statements)

options.text: NSAIDs as sole analgesia, no antibiotic cover and observation alone with no transfusion or escalation, given hydroxyurea adherence is documented in this patient with sickle cell disease
options.rationale: Acute chest syndrome demands antibiotic cover, oxygen and consideration of transfusion regardless of hydroxyurea status. (Source: BSH sickle cell disease guideline)

options.text: Continuous positive airway pressure as the only intervention without any antibiotic therapy, opioid analgesia, transfusion plan or incentive spirometry while admitted on the medical ward
options.rationale: Non-invasive ventilation is an adjunct; antibiotics, opioid analgesia and transfusion are core therapy. (Source: BSH sickle cell disease guideline)

options.text: Oxygen, intravenous fluids at maintenance, opioid analgesia, intravenous ceftriaxone plus oral azithromycin, incentive spirometry, simple top-up or exchange transfusion, haematology and ICU review
options.rationale: Acute chest syndrome is managed with oxygen, judicious fluids, opioid analgesia, broad-spectrum antibiotics covering typical and atypical pathogens, incentive spirometry, and simple top-up transfusion (or exchange transfusion for severe disease), with haematology and ICU involvement. (Source: BSH sickle cell disease guideline)

options.text: Single high-dose acute hydroxyurea bolus as the immediate therapy without any other measures, on the basis that hydroxyurea is the cornerstone of long-term disease modification in this patient
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea is long-term prevention; it does not rescue acute chest syndrome. (Source: BSH sickle cell disease guideline)

explanation.summary: Acute chest syndrome is a new pulmonary infiltrate with respiratory symptoms in sickle cell disease. Mortality in adults is around 5 to 10 percent. Management is oxygen to maintain saturation above 95 percent, judicious crystalloid (avoiding overload), opioid analgesia, broad-spectrum antibiotics covering typical and atypical organisms (eg ceftriaxone plus azithromycin), bronchodilators if reactive airways, incentive spirometry, and simple top-up transfusion or exchange transfusion for severe disease. Haematology and ICU involvement is essential. Triggers (infection, hypoventilation, fat embolism, atelectasis) are sought and addressed.
key_points: Acute chest syndrome: new infiltrate plus respiratory symptoms in sickle cell disease
Broad-spectrum antibiotics cover typical and atypical pathogens
Exchange transfusion is reserved for severe disease (hypoxia, falling Hb, multi-lobar involvement)
explanation.pearls: Adult acute chest syndrome mortality drops with early ceftriaxone plus azithromycin, incentive spirometry every hour while awake, and exchange transfusion when SpO2 falls below 90 percent on supplemental oxygen.

sources.label: HSANZ sickle cell disease position statement

stem: A 72-year-old man with non-valvular atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5 mg twice daily for stroke prevention is brought to ED with sudden dense left hemiparesis 90 minutes after his most recent dose. CT brain shows a 22 mL right basal ganglia haematoma without intraventricular extension or imminent herniation. His baseline INR was 1.0; he is on no antiplatelet therapy. He has no documented liver or significant renal disease. He is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Examination: Left-sided hemiparesis affecting face, arm and leg; dysarthria; no signs of herniation
data_table.Investigations: CT brain: 22 mL right basal ganglia ICH, no IVH; platelets 218 x10^9/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, INR 1.1, anti-Xa raised (consistent with on-therapy DOAC)

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line reversal strategy?

options.text: Transfusion of 4 units of fresh frozen plasma alone with no additional agent, expecting correction within 60 minutes of the start of transfusion in this DOAC-associated intracranial haemorrhage
options.rationale: FFP does not reverse direct factor Xa inhibitors; specific reversal is required. (Source: THANZ position statement on anticoagulant reversal; AHA/ASA spontaneous ICH guideline)

options.text: Vitamin K 10 mg intravenously alone without any other agent, expecting full reversal of apixaban anticoagulant effect within 6 to 12 hours after administration in this patient
options.rationale: Vitamin K reverses warfarin, not DOACs; not the agent of choice here. (Source: THANZ anticoagulant reversal statement)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g intravenous infusion as the sole haemostatic agent with no specific DOAC reversal, no factor concentrate and no further intervention beyond supportive care for this DOAC-associated intracranial haemorrhage
options.rationale: TXA is not a substitute for specific reversal in DOAC ICH; outcome trials show minimal effect on functional outcome. (Source: THANZ anticoagulant reversal statement; TICH-2)

options.text: Defer reversal for 12 hours to allow natural drug clearance, with neurosurgical reassessment only if clinical deterioration occurs and no additional haemostatic intervention is undertaken now in this patient
options.rationale: Active intracerebral bleeding within an apixaban dosing interval requires immediate reversal, not observation. (Source: THANZ anticoagulant reversal statement)

options.text: Andexanet alfa if available, otherwise 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate 50 IU/kg with BP control, neurosurgical referral and reversal of any concurrent antiplatelet therapy
options.rationale: Specific factor Xa reversal with andexanet alfa, or 4F-PCC 50 IU/kg when andexanet is unavailable, is the AU pathway for DOAC ICH, alongside BP control under 140 systolic and neurosurgical referral. (Source: THANZ anticoagulant reversal statement; AHA/ASA spontaneous ICH guideline)

explanation.summary: Anticoagulant-associated intracranial haemorrhage requires emergent reversal with the agent specific to the anticoagulant. For factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban), andexanet alfa is the targeted reversal agent where available; otherwise 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate 50 IU/kg is the alternative. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran. Concurrent BP control to 140 systolic, neurosurgical referral for haematoma evacuation candidates, and reversal of antiplatelet therapy with platelet transfusion (selective, in operative cases) complete the package.
key_points: Andexanet alfa reverses apixaban and rivaroxaban; 4F-PCC 50 IU/kg is the alternative
Idarucizumab is the specific reversal agent for dabigatran
BP target under 140 systolic and neurosurgical referral are concurrent priorities
explanation.pearls: In apixaban-associated ICH, andexanet alfa 400 to 800 mg IV bolus followed by infusion (or 4F-PCC 50 IU/kg when andexanet unavailable), with BP lowered to under 140 systolic, halves haematoma expansion compared with no specific reversal.
